topline: Today's headlines, led by: US Senate Passes War Powers Resolution to Halt Iran Conflict, Rebuking Trump.

stories.headline: US Senate Passes War Powers Resolution to Halt Iran Conflict, Rebuking Trump
stories.what: The US Senate has for the first time approved a War Powers resolution aimed at halting the American military conflict with Iran, delivering a significant bipartisan rebuke to President Donald Trump. The resolution signals Congress's assertion of its constitutional authority over war-making, a rare check on executive power. The move comes amid deep public scepticism about the conflict, with polls showing only 24% of Americans believe the war with Iran was worth it.
stories.lens: A US Congress that reins in American military action in the Middle East directly shapes the geopolitical environment India navigates — from oil supply routes to its own diplomatic balancing act between Washington and Tehran.
stories.article:
The US Senate passed a War Powers resolution to halt the American military conflict with Iran, marking the first time Congress has ever approved such a measure — a direct and historic rebuke to President Donald Trump's conduct of the war.

The resolution invokes the War Powers Act, a post-Vietnam law designed to limit the president's ability to commit US forces to combat without congressional authorisation. Its passage reflects rare bipartisan concern on Capitol Hill about the scope and legality of Trump's military engagement with Iran, and represents a significant assertion of legislative authority over foreign policy.

Public opinion appears to be driving some of the congressional momentum. Polls indicate that only 24% of Americans believe the war with Iran was worth fighting — a remarkably low figure that suggests the conflict has limited popular support even among those who may have initially backed a tough stance on Tehran.

The Trump administration has separately been touting an Iran deal as a potential boon for US farmers, though Tehran has denied the existence of any such arrangement, adding diplomatic murkiness to an already volatile situation.

For India, the stakes are considerable. The Iran conflict threatens regional stability in a zone through which a significant share of India's energy imports travel, and New Delhi — which has historically maintained ties with both Washington and Tehran — will be watching the congressional-executive standoff closely as it calibrates its own foreign policy position.
key_facts: First-ever War Powers resolution approved by US Senate
Resolution rebukes Trump's conduct of war with Iran
Only 24% of Americans polled believe the Iran war was worth it
Tehran denies Trump admin claims of an Iran deal benefiting US farmers

stories.headline: Bill Gates Tells US Congress That Jeffrey Epstein Considered Blackmailing Him
stories.what: Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates testified before the US Congress, revealing that Jeffrey Epstein had considered blackmailing him. The disclosure marks one of the most significant public statements Gates has made about his relationship with the convicted sex offender, and comes as Congress continues to examine the full extent of Epstein's network of influence and leverage over prominent figures. Gates did not elaborate on the nature of the potential blackmail threat in the reported account.
stories.lens: Gates's congressional testimony about Epstein's blackmail consideration adds a striking new dimension to one of the most consequential influence-and-power scandals of recent decades, with implications for how elite networks are scrutinised globally.
stories.article:
Bill Gates, the Microsoft co-founder and one of the world's most prominent philanthropists, told the US Congress that Jeffrey Epstein had considered blackmailing him, in a revelation that marks the most direct public statement Gates has made about the nature of his dealings with the convicted sex trafficker.

The testimony represents a significant moment in the ongoing congressional effort to map the full extent of Epstein's influence network. Epstein, who died in a federal jail in 2019 in circumstances officially ruled a suicide, was known to have cultivated relationships with some of the world's most powerful figures in business, politics and academia — and investigators have long sought to understand how he used that access.

Gates had previously faced intense public scrutiny over the extent and nature of his meetings with Epstein, particularly after reports emerged that the two had met multiple times even after Epstein's 2008 sex-offender conviction. Gates had previously described those meetings as a mistake.

The revelation that Epstein actively considered using blackmail as leverage against Gates adds a darker layer to what was already a deeply troubling relationship. It also raises questions about how many others in Epstein's orbit may have been subject to similar calculations — and whether those threats were ever acted upon. Congressional investigators are expected to press further on the details of Gates's testimony.
key_facts: Bill Gates testified before US Congress about Jeffrey Epstein
Gates said Epstein had considered blackmailing him
Epstein died in federal custody in 2019
Gates had previously called his meetings with Epstein a mistake
stories.image_credit: Bogdan Hoyaux / European Union / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Women Targeted With Date-Rape Drugs and Stabbings at France's Music Night
stories.what: Women attending France's annual Fête de la Musique — a nationwide music celebration — were targeted with date-rape drugs and stabbings in disturbing incidents that have shocked the country. The attacks highlight a persistent and serious problem of violence against women at large public events in France, and have triggered outrage and calls for stronger security measures. The Fête de la Musique, held every year on June 21st, draws millions of people to street concerts across the country.
stories.lens: Violence against women at public festivals is a global concern, and France's high-profile incidents serve as a stark reminder of the safety challenges that persist even at celebrated civic events — a conversation equally relevant in India.
stories.article:
Women attending France's Fête de la Musique were targeted with date-rape drugs and stabbings during the country's beloved annual music celebration, in incidents that have provoked widespread outrage and renewed debate about the safety of women at public gatherings in France.

The Fête de la Musique, held every year on June 21st to mark the summer solstice, is one of France's most cherished cultural traditions — a night when streets across the country fill with free concerts and spontaneous performances. This year's celebrations were marred by multiple reports of women being drugged and attacked, casting a shadow over an event normally associated with joy and community.

The incidents have triggered immediate calls from women's rights groups and politicians for stronger security measures at public events, better policing, and faster responses to reports of drink-spiking and assault. France has grappled with the issue of needle-spiking and drink-spiking at public venues for several years, with previous festivals and nightclubs also reporting clusters of attacks.

The violence underscores a broader European pattern of harassment and assault targeting women at large outdoor events, a problem that has proved difficult to eradicate despite increased awareness and occasional security crackdowns. For many French women, the incidents represent a continuation of a deeply troubling trend that limits their ability to participate freely in public life.
key_facts: Women attacked with date-rape drugs and stabbings at Fête de la Musique
France's annual music night draws millions to street concerts nationwide
Incidents have sparked outrage and calls for stronger security
France has faced recurring reports of drink-spiking at public events
stories.image_credit: dalbera from Paris, France / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: JD Vance Proposed Indian and Saudi Troops as Ukraine Peacekeepers — Trump Rejected It
stories.what: US Vice President JD Vance proposed deploying Indian and Saudi Arabian troops as peacekeepers in Ukraine as part of a potential ceasefire framework, according to a new book. President Donald Trump reportedly rejected the idea outright, with Trump said to have responded that India and Saudi Arabia "won't do that." The revelation sheds light on internal White House deliberations over how to end the Russia-Ukraine conflict and the roles envisaged for major non-Western powers.
stories.lens: The proposal — even in its rejected form — signals that Washington sees India as a potential security actor in European conflicts, a role New Delhi has carefully avoided, making this a significant diplomatic data point for Indian foreign policy.
stories.article:
US Vice President JD Vance privately suggested that Indian and Saudi Arabian troops be deployed as peacekeepers in Ukraine as part of an effort to broker a ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine war, according to a new book detailing internal White House deliberations. President Donald Trump rejected the proposal, reportedly saying that India and Saudi Arabia simply "won't do that."

The revelation offers a rare window into the Trump administration's internal debates over Ukraine policy and the creative — if ultimately rejected — options being floated to end Europe's most devastating conflict in decades. Vance's suggestion reflects a broader US interest in bringing in neutral or non-aligned powers to provide credibility to any peacekeeping arrangement, given the deep mistrust between Russia and NATO member states.

For India, the story carries particular diplomatic weight. New Delhi has consistently maintained a position of strategic autonomy on the Ukraine conflict — abstaining on key UN votes, continuing trade with Russia, and positioning itself as a potential mediator rather than a participant. The idea that Washington was even internally discussing a role for Indian troops underlines the expectations some in the US government have of India as a rising global security actor.

India has shown no public appetite for such a deployment, and the government's stated position emphasises dialogue and diplomacy over military involvement. Trump's swift dismissal of the idea may have spared New Delhi an awkward conversation — but the episode is unlikely to be the last time India's potential role in the Ukraine conflict is raised in global capitals.
key_facts: Vance proposed Indian and Saudi troops as Ukraine peacekeepers
Trump rejected the proposal, saying they 'won't do that'
Revelation comes from a new book on White House deliberations
India has maintained strategic autonomy on the Russia-Ukraine conflict

stories.headline: Crimea Locks Down as Putin Acknowledges 'Huge Stream' of Ukrainian Drones
stories.what: Russia has placed Crimea under lockdown conditions as President Vladimir Putin publicly acknowledged a "huge stream" of Ukrainian drones targeting the peninsula. The admission is notable for its candour from the Kremlin, which has often downplayed Ukrainian military successes. The drone campaign represents a sustained Ukrainian effort to strike Russian-held territory and disrupt military logistics, infrastructure and morale in the occupied region.
stories.lens: Escalation over Crimea — a strategically vital peninsula Russia annexed in 2014 — raises the risk of a broader escalation in the war that would reverberate through global energy markets and India's carefully maintained neutrality.
stories.article:
Russia placed Crimea under lockdown as President Vladimir Putin acknowledged a "huge stream" of Ukrainian drones striking the occupied peninsula, in a rare public admission from the Kremlin about the scale of Ukraine's aerial campaign against Russian-held territory.

The lockdown in Crimea — which Russia seized and annexed from Ukraine in 2014 — signals the seriousness with which Moscow views the drone threat. Ukraine has escalated its long-range drone and missile campaign in recent months, targeting military installations, supply routes, and infrastructure in Crimea as part of a broader strategy to pressure Russian forces and complicate their logistics.

Putin's acknowledgement of a "huge stream" of drones is a departure from the Kremlin's usual communications posture, which tends to minimise Ukrainian military achievements. The comment suggests the drone campaign is causing genuine disruption, and may also be intended to justify continued Russian military operations to a domestic audience.

The intensification of fighting over Crimea adds a new flashpoint to an already volatile war at a moment when US congressional action — including the passage of a War Powers resolution on Iran — is reshaping the international context for how Washington manages multiple military engagements simultaneously. For India, which imports energy and maintains diplomatic channels with both sides, the continued escalation in Ukraine is a source of ongoing strategic concern.
key_facts: Crimea placed under lockdown amid intensifying Ukrainian drone strikes
Putin publicly admitted a 'huge stream' of Ukrainian drones
Ukraine has escalated its aerial campaign targeting Russian-held territory
Crimea has been under Russian occupation since 2014

stories.headline: India–US trade deal may be signed before July 24 as Washington calls it 'very, very close'
stories.what: A landmark trade agreement between India and the United States could be signed before July 24, with Washington describing the deal as 'very, very close'. Negotiations have entered their final stretch, with both sides reportedly having resolved several key sticking points. The deal, if concluded, would be one of the most significant bilateral trade agreements India has signed in recent years, covering tariffs, market access and regulatory standards across multiple sectors.
stories.lens: A concluded India-US trade deal would directly affect prices of American goods in India, open new export markets for Indian manufacturers and set the tone for India's trade posture in a shifting global order.
stories.article:
India and the United States are on the verge of a historic trade agreement, with Washington signalling the deal is 'very, very close' and reports pointing to a possible signing before July 24. The development marks the culmination of months of intensive negotiations that have covered tariffs, non-tariff barriers, digital trade and agricultural market access.

Both governments have faced domestic pressures throughout the talks. Washington has pushed for greater access to India's agricultural and dairy markets, while New Delhi has sought lower US tariffs on labour-intensive exports such as textiles, gems and jewellery, and a more predictable framework for its IT services sector. The closeness of a deal suggests significant ground has been covered on both sides.

The July 24 deadline carries symbolic weight — it marks 90 days from the point at which the US announced a pause on reciprocal tariffs, a window seen as the de facto negotiating clock for several countries simultaneously pursuing agreements with Washington. Missing that window could mean returning to a higher-tariff environment, adding urgency to the final push.

For Indian markets and businesses, the implications are broad. Sectors from pharmaceuticals and IT to auto components and agriculture will all be recalibrated by whatever terms emerge. Analysts have noted that even a partial or framework agreement would boost investor sentiment and give Indian exporters greater certainty at a time when global supply chains are being rapidly redrawn.
key_facts: India-US trade deal described as 'very, very close' by Washington
Possible signing before July 24
Deal covers tariffs, market access, digital trade and more
July 24 aligns with 90-day US tariff pause deadline

stories.headline: NITI Aayog launches eighth edition of Trade Watch Quarterly
stories.what: NITI Aayog has released the eighth edition of its 'Trade Watch Quarterly', a flagship publication that tracks India's merchandise and services trade trends, export performance across sectors and the country's evolving position in global value chains. The report provides policymakers, businesses and researchers with a data-rich assessment of where India's trade is gaining momentum and where gaps remain. Its release comes at a particularly significant moment, with India-US trade negotiations entering a decisive phase.
stories.lens: Trade Watch Quarterly is one of the government's key tools for benchmarking India's export ambitions — at a time when trade deals and global supply chain shifts could define India's economic growth decade.
stories.article:
NITI Aayog has published the eighth edition of its Trade Watch Quarterly, the government think tank's regular deep-dive into India's trade performance across goods, services and key partner economies. The publication serves as an authoritative reference point for tracking whether India is on course to meet its ambitious export targets and where structural bottlenecks persist.

The timing of the eighth edition is notable. India is in the final stages of negotiating a landmark bilateral trade agreement with the United States, and global supply chains are being restructured at pace as companies diversify away from China. Understanding where India's export competitiveness is strongest — and where it lags — is therefore a practical priority for both government planners and private sector decision-makers.

Trade Watch Quarterly typically covers India's performance in merchandise categories such as engineering goods, pharmaceuticals, textiles and agricultural products, as well as services exports including IT and business process management. It also tracks India's import composition, which has significant implications for the current account and rupee stability.

For the business community, the publication is a useful compass in a rapidly shifting global trade environment. With the India-US deal potentially imminent and conversations around free trade agreements with other partners continuing, the analytical framework provided by Trade Watch Quarterly will help frame where new market access would have the greatest economic impact.
key_facts: NITI Aayog releases eighth edition of Trade Watch Quarterly
Publication tracks India's goods and services trade performance
Release coincides with final stage of India-US trade negotiations
Covers engineering goods, pharma, textiles, IT services exports

stories.headline: Sensex rebounds 291 points as GIFT Nifty signals positive open despite Wall Street tech sell-off
stories.what: Indian equity markets opened positively on June 24, with the Sensex rebounding 291 points, even as Wall Street's tech-heavy indices suffered a sell-off overnight. GIFT Nifty had signalled a higher opening, and softening crude oil prices provided an additional tailwind for domestic markets. The divergence between India's resilience and the US tech correction reflects growing confidence in India's relative macro stability.
stories.lens: A 291-point Sensex rebound and cheaper crude oil directly benefit Indian investors and ease inflation pressures for ordinary consumers who feel every rupee of fuel price movement.
stories.article:
Indian stock markets shrugged off a Nasdaq sell-off on Wall Street to open higher on June 24, with the Sensex posting a rebound of 291 points. GIFT Nifty had flagged the positive start in early-morning trade, and falling crude oil prices gave domestic sentiment an additional lift heading into the session.

The Wall Street tech correction, which rattled US markets overnight, had raised concerns about a spillover into Indian equities. However, traders noted that India's market has been increasingly able to decouple from short-term US moves, particularly when domestic macro factors — such as softening energy prices — are moving in a favourable direction. Cheaper crude is a significant positive for India, which imports the vast majority of its oil needs, as it eases the current account deficit and dampens fuel-driven inflation.

Among the stocks on watch during the session were Infosys, IRFC, Honasa Consumer, Rashi Peripherals, City Union Bank and Delhivery, reflecting a broad spread of sectors from IT and infrastructure finance to consumer goods and logistics. Analysts pointed to a mixed global cue environment but said domestic institutional buying was helping to underpin the indices.

The session's direction will also be watched against the backdrop of macro risks flagged earlier in the week, including the potential for El Niño to stoke food inflation later in the year. For now, however, markets appeared to be taking comfort from lower energy costs and the prospect of a near-term India-US trade agreement, both of which are seen as broadly positive for corporate earnings.
key_facts: Sensex rebounded 291 points on June 24
GIFT Nifty signalled positive open despite Nasdaq sell-off
Softening crude oil prices supported Indian market sentiment
Stocks to watch included Infosys, IRFC, Honasa Consumer, Delhivery

stories.headline: Markets brace for inflation risk as India weighs El Niño threat to agriculture
stories.what: Indian financial markets are factoring in the risk that an El Niño weather pattern could disrupt the monsoon and drive up food inflation, adding a macro headache for policymakers already navigating global uncertainty. Analysts warn that a below-normal monsoon triggered by El Niño could push vegetable and cereal prices sharply higher, complicating the Reserve Bank of India's rate trajectory. India has historically been vulnerable to El Niño-driven agricultural shocks, given its dependence on a well-distributed monsoon for kharif crop production.
stories.lens: For ordinary Indians, El Niño-driven food inflation hits hardest in grocery bills — and if it forces the RBI to hold rates higher for longer, it also keeps home loan EMIs elevated.
stories.article:
Indian markets are beginning to price in a new risk factor: the possibility that an El Niño weather event could undermine the 2025 monsoon, crimp agricultural output and reignite food inflation at a time when policymakers are hoping to ease monetary conditions. The concern is not hypothetical — India's inflation history shows a clear pattern of food price spikes in El Niño years, driven by reduced rainfall in key farming regions.

The implications for monetary policy are significant. The Reserve Bank of India has been cautiously navigating a path toward easier rates, but a sustained surge in food prices — particularly vegetables, pulses and cereals — could force a rethink. Markets are acutely aware that food carries a heavy weight in India's consumer price index, meaning weather-driven supply shocks translate quickly into headline inflation numbers.

For equity markets, the concern plays out in multiple ways. Consumption-oriented sectors — fast-moving consumer goods, rural retail, two-wheelers — are sensitive to rural income shocks, which tend to follow poor harvests. At the same time, higher food inflation can dampen urban consumption by squeezing household budgets.

Meteorological agencies have flagged developing El Niño conditions, though the full impact on the Indian monsoon remains uncertain. Analysts say the market's current posture is one of watchful caution rather than alarm — but if early-season rainfall data disappoints, the inflation risk narrative could move quickly from background concern to front-and-centre market driver.
key_facts: El Niño conditions flagged as risk to India's monsoon
Potential food inflation spike could complicate RBI rate decisions
Food has heavy weighting in India's consumer price index
Rural consumption sectors most exposed to agricultural shock
stories.image_credit: Regional Meteorological Center, New Delhi (India Meteorological Department) / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Vaibhav Sooryavanshi at INR 35 crore, Rishabh Pant-Kuldeep trade: IPL 2027's value paradox laid bare
stories.what: The IPL's transfer market has thrown up a striking value paradox ahead of the 2027 season: Delhi Capitals and Lucknow Super Giants have completed a high-profile trade involving Rishabh Pant and Kuldeep Yadav, while the retention of teenager Vaibhav Sooryavanshi at INR 35 crore is drawing scrutiny as a potential overpay. The deals together expose a widening gap in how IPL franchises value experience versus youth potential, and the distortions that mega-auction dynamics can create.
stories.lens: The INR 35 crore price tag on a teenager and the Pant-Kuldeep swap show how IPL franchise valuations and bidding wars are reshaping Indian cricket's transfer economy — with consequences for the national team's squad balance.
stories.article:
The IPL's transfer activity ahead of the 2027 season has generated significant debate, with two deals in particular laying bare the league's most glaring value tensions. Delhi Capitals and Lucknow Super Giants have formalised a trade involving Rishabh Pant and Kuldeep Yadav — two of Indian cricket's most high-profile names — in a swap that reshuffles the balance of power in both squads.

At the same time, the retention of Vaibhav Sooryavanshi at INR 35 crore is drawing scrutiny from analysts and former players alike. Sooryavanshi's talent is widely acknowledged, but the price point — applied to a teenager yet to play a full IPL season — is being cited as a cautionary example of the auction inflation that the IPL's mega-retention system can produce.

The Pant-Kuldeep trade is itself a significant strategic call. Pant, one of the most explosive batting talents in the game, and Kuldeep Yadav, whose wrist-spin has been central to India's recent white-ball success, represent very different assets. How each fits into the fabric of their new franchise will be one of the storylines of IPL 2027.

Taken together, the two deals illustrate what critics are calling the IPL's biggest value gap: the mismatch between what franchises pay and what they demonstrably get. With broadcast revenues and franchise valuations at all-time highs, the pressure to retain marquee names — regardless of cost-efficiency — appears to be growing, raising questions about long-term squad-building sustainability.
key_facts: Vaibhav Sooryavanshi retained at INR 35 crore
Delhi Capitals and Lucknow Super Giants complete Pant-Kuldeep trade
Deal highlights IPL franchise value distortions ahead of 2027
Rishabh Pant and Kuldeep Yadav are the players in the swap

stories.headline: Couple returns after 15 years in the US to build startups in Bengaluru
stories.what: A couple who spent 15 years working in the United States have returned to India to build startups in Bengaluru, joining a growing wave of reverse migration among Indian tech professionals. Their decision reflects rising confidence in India's startup infrastructure, talent pool, and market opportunity, as well as a shifting calculus around quality of life and professional impact. The story, reported by NDTV, highlights a trend that India's innovation ecosystem is increasingly counting on to bring back experienced talent.
stories.lens: Reverse migration of experienced Indian tech professionals from the US directly enriches Bengaluru's startup ecosystem with global expertise and risk-taking capital, which benefits the broader innovation economy.
stories.article:
After spending 15 years building careers in the United States, a couple has returned to Bengaluru to launch their own startups — a choice that puts them at the centre of one of the more quietly significant trends reshaping India's technology landscape. NDTV reported their story as emblematic of a broader reverse-migration movement gathering pace among Indian professionals who once saw the US as the only arena for serious ambition.

The reasons driving this kind of return are multiple. India's startup ecosystem has matured dramatically over the past decade, with Bengaluru in particular offering world-class co-working infrastructure, a deep pool of engineering talent, and access to a domestic consumer market of continental scale. For founders, the combination of lower burn rates, local market knowledge, and the prestige that now attaches to building in India makes the case for returning increasingly compelling.

There is also a pull factor rooted in a sense of timing. Many returning entrepreneurs believe they are catching India at an inflection point — a moment when the country's digital infrastructure, from UPI to cloud penetration, has made it possible to build category-defining companies that simply could not have existed a decade ago. Fifteen years of US experience in product development, fundraising norms, and scaling operations is seen as a distinct advantage when brought back to an Indian context.

For Bengaluru, each such return adds to a compounding effect: experienced founders mentor others, create employment, and signal to global investors that the city can retain and attract serious talent. Whether this couple's ventures succeed or not, their choice itself is the story — and it is one India's startup establishment is eager to tell.
key_facts: Couple returned to Bengaluru after 15 years working in the US
They are building startups in Bengaluru's tech ecosystem
Part of a broader reverse-migration trend among Indian tech professionals

stories.headline: SEBI's 'GARUDA' framework eases key bottlenecks for Indian startup investors
stories.what: The Securities and Exchange Board of India has introduced the 'GARUDA' framework, designed to ease critical bottlenecks that have long frustrated investors in Indian startups. The framework addresses structural pain points in the domestic startup investment ecosystem, including issues around fund structures, compliance, and capital access for early-stage companies. The move is seen as a significant regulatory step to make India more competitive as a destination for startup capital.
stories.lens: Easier investment rules under GARUDA could unlock more domestic and foreign capital for Indian startups, directly affecting the pace of innovation and job creation in the sector.
stories.article:
India's market regulator SEBI has rolled out a new framework called GARUDA, aimed at removing longstanding structural barriers that have made it cumbersome for investors to back Indian startups. The announcement, reported by Inc42, marks one of the more consequential regulatory interventions in the startup investment space in recent years.

Investors in Indian startups — both domestic venture funds and foreign institutional players — have repeatedly flagged friction points around fund registration, compliance timelines, and restrictions on certain investment structures as impediments to deploying capital efficiently. The GARUDA framework is positioned as a direct response to this feedback, though the specific provisions will govern how investor-friendly the changes prove in practice.

The timing is significant. India's startup ecosystem has been navigating a prolonged period of tighter capital availability following the funding boom of 2021-22. Regulatory easing at this juncture could provide a meaningful tailwind, encouraging funds that have been sitting on the sidelines — particularly in early and growth-stage investing — to move more actively into Indian deals.

For founders, a more liquid investment environment would ease the pressure that has forced many startups to cut costs and delay expansion. SEBI's willingness to engage constructively with the startup ecosystem's needs also signals a maturing relationship between the regulator and the innovation economy, one that could shape how India's next generation of technology companies gets built and funded.
key_facts: SEBI introduced the 'GARUDA' framework for startup investment
Framework targets key bottlenecks for investors in Indian startups
Aims to improve fund structures, compliance, and capital access
stories.image_credit: Securities Exchange Board of India / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Tech-related hiring in India at all-time low, software engineers hit hardest: Study
stories.what: Tech hiring in India has fallen to an all-time low, with software engineers bearing the brunt of the downturn, according to a new study reported by India Today. The slump reflects a combination of global tech sector caution, widespread adoption of AI tools that reduce the need for large engineering teams, and a pullback by both Indian IT giants and startups on fresh recruitment. The findings raise serious concerns about employment prospects for the millions of engineering graduates entering the workforce each year.
stories.lens: For India's vast pool of engineering graduates and working software professionals, an all-time low in tech hiring is a direct threat to livelihoods and career prospects in the country's most aspirational sector.
stories.article:
Tech-related hiring in India has hit an all-time low, with software engineers the worst-affected group, according to a study cited by India Today. The finding crystallises what job-seekers and placement officers at engineering colleges have been experiencing on the ground — a sharp, sustained contraction in the demand for technical talent that shows no immediate signs of reversing.

The causes are multiple and reinforcing. Large Indian IT services companies have been cautious about headcount expansion as clients in the US and Europe tighten discretionary technology spending. At the same time, the rapid adoption of AI-powered coding tools and automation has reduced the number of entry-level and mid-tier software engineering roles that companies need to fill. Startups, which drove a hiring boom in earlier years, have also pulled back sharply following a funding winter that began in 2022 and has yet to fully lift.

Software engineers — the traditional backbone of India's technology workforce — are disproportionately affected. Roles in areas like basic coding, testing, and routine application development, which once absorbed hundreds of thousands of graduates annually, are contracting most visibly. More specialised roles in AI, machine learning, and cybersecurity are somewhat more resilient, but they represent a far smaller share of the overall market.

The findings arrive at a difficult moment for India's higher education system, which produces a large number of engineering graduates each year. Unless the hiring cycle turns or new sectors absorb displaced talent, the gap between the supply of technically trained graduates and available jobs is set to widen further.
key_facts: Tech hiring in India is at an all-time low per the study
Software engineers are the hardest-hit group
AI adoption and startup funding winter cited as contributing factors

stories.headline: Will a nation grow, stagnate, decline? Technology will tell
stories.what: A major analysis in The Indian Express argues that a nation's trajectory — growth, stagnation, or decline — is increasingly determined by its technology adoption and innovation capacity rather than traditional factors like geography or natural resources. The piece draws on global examples to make the case that countries which invest in and deploy technology effectively pull ahead, while those that lag fall behind in living standards, economic output, and geopolitical influence. For India, the argument has direct relevance as the country positions itself for the next phase of development.
stories.lens: As India competes to move up the global economic ladder, the argument that technology is now the primary determinant of national destiny puts pressure on policymakers to accelerate digital investment and reform.
stories.article:
Technology has become the master variable of national destiny — more predictive of a country's future than its demographics, natural endowments, or even its governance structures. That is the central argument of a sweeping analysis published by The Indian Express, which examines how the divergence between technology-forward nations and laggards is accelerating in ways that may soon be irreversible.

The piece points to the compounding nature of technological advantage: countries that invest early in digital infrastructure, AI capability, and STEM education generate productivity gains that allow them to invest further, creating a virtuous cycle. Conversely, nations that delay or deprioritise technology adoption find the catch-up task growing harder with each passing year, as the frontier moves faster than their capacity to close the gap.

For India, the analysis arrives at a moment of genuine choice. The country has demonstrated its ability to build large-scale digital public infrastructure — Aadhaar, UPI, and the ONDC network are globally cited examples — but translating that foundation into broad-based productivity improvement across agriculture, manufacturing, and services remains a work in progress. The next five years are widely seen as decisive for whether India locks in a high-growth technology trajectory or settles into a middle-income equilibrium.

The broader message — that technology is no longer a sector but a civilisational operating system — has implications for how India allocates its education budgets, structures its trade policy, and engages with global technology governance. Nations that grasp this shift and act on it, the analysis suggests, will grow. Those that do not, will not.
key_facts: Technology adoption now seen as primary driver of national growth or decline
India's digital infrastructure (UPI, Aadhaar) cited as a foundation to build on
Next five years framed as decisive for India's technology trajectory

stories.headline: India's MoEngage bets the future of marketing is millions of AI agents
stories.what: Bengaluru-based marketing technology company MoEngage is pivoting its platform around the idea that the future of customer engagement will be driven by millions of autonomous AI agents rather than traditional campaign tools. The company is building out infrastructure for AI agents that can individually tailor marketing interactions at scale, replacing the manual segmentation and scheduling that marketers rely on today. MoEngage's bet represents a significant strategic shift for one of India's more prominent B2B SaaS companies.
stories.lens: If AI-agent-driven marketing becomes the norm, it could reshape India's fast-growing SaaS and martech industry while changing how brands from small businesses to large corporations communicate with customers.
stories.article:
MoEngage, the Bengaluru-headquartered marketing technology platform, is making a major strategic wager: that the next era of customer engagement will be built on millions of AI agents operating simultaneously, each one personalising interactions for individual consumers at a scale no human team could match. The vision, reported by TechCrunch, positions MoEngage at the frontier of a fundamental shift in how businesses think about marketing automation.

Traditional marketing platforms, including earlier versions of MoEngage itself, work by having teams define audience segments, craft campaigns, and schedule messages. The AI-agent model inverts this — instead of marketers setting rules, autonomous agents continuously learn from user behaviour and independently decide what to communicate, when, and through which channel. For brands managing millions of customers across push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app messaging, the efficiency gains could be dramatic.

MoEngage already serves a large roster of enterprise and mid-market clients across Asia, the Middle East, and beyond. The company's move into AI agents is both a product evolution and a competitive necessity, as global martech giants and well-funded startups race to embed AI more deeply into their platforms. Being an Indian company with deep roots in emerging-market consumer behaviour gives MoEngage a potentially distinctive angle on how to build agents that work for high-volume, price-sensitive markets.

The broader implication is that India's SaaS sector — long seen as a cost-effective back-office for global businesses — is increasingly shaping the cutting edge of enterprise software. MoEngage's AI-agent bet is a signal that Indian product companies are no longer following trends but trying to set them.
key_facts: MoEngage is building a platform around millions of autonomous AI agents
The strategy replaces traditional manual marketing segmentation
MoEngage is a Bengaluru-based B2B SaaS/martech company

stories.headline: CJI Surya Kant: Technology Can Help Courts, But Justice Must Stay Human
stories.what: Chief Justice of India Surya Kant has stated that while technology can assist the judicial system, justice remains fundamentally a human endeavour. The CJI also separately noted that courts face the pressing challenge of preserving public trust in the justice system. His remarks come at a time when the judiciary is actively exploring AI and digital tools to reduce case backlogs, but faces questions about the limits of automation in adjudication.
stories.lens: With India's courts burdened by millions of pending cases and under pressure to adopt AI tools, the CJI's caution that justice cannot be reduced to an algorithm sets an important guardrail for how far digital transformation in courts should go.
stories.article:
Chief Justice of India Surya Kant has delivered a clear message on the future of judicial technology: digital tools are welcome aids, but the act of delivering justice must remain a human one. Speaking at a recent event, the CJI emphasised that while technology could improve efficiency and access within the court system, the nuanced, empathetic, and morally weighted nature of judicial decisions could not be delegated to machines.

The CJI also flagged a broader institutional concern, warning that courts today face the challenge of preserving public trust — a remark that carries weight at a time when the judiciary is under scrutiny over pendency, accessibility, and accountability. His comments suggest an awareness at the highest levels of the judiciary that the courts' legitimacy rests not only on their legal authority but on the confidence citizens place in them.

India's courts are currently exploring a range of technological interventions, from AI-assisted case management and virtual hearings to automated scheduling systems. While these tools have helped ease some administrative burdens, debates continue about whether algorithmic assistance in legal research or judgment-drafting crosses a line that should not be crossed.

CJI Surya Kant's remarks are likely to shape the ongoing conversation among judges, lawyers, and policymakers about where technology ends and the irreplaceable human judgment of a judge must begin — a distinction with profound consequences for how millions of Indians experience justice.
key_facts: CJI Surya Kant said technology can aid courts but justice is a human endeavour
CJI also warned courts face a challenge of preserving public trust
Remarks come amid judiciary's active adoption of digital and AI tools
India's courts carry a massive backlog of pending cases
stories.image_credit: Supreme Court of India / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Tamil Nadu Government Rushes to Supreme Court Over Karthigai Deepam Row
stories.what: The Tamil Nadu government, led by Chief Minister M.K. Stalin's DMK administration, has approached the Supreme Court challenging a Madras High Court order related to the Karthigai Deepam festival dispute. The row centres on the lighting of the sacred flame atop Thiruvannamalai's Arunachaleswarar temple, entangled in questions of faith, property rights, and public order. The state government's urgent appeal signals the high stakes of the dispute for both religious communities and governance.
stories.lens: The Karthigai Deepam row pits state authority against religious tradition and property rights at one of Tamil Nadu's most sacred festivals, setting up a landmark Supreme Court ruling on how governments can manage temple rituals.
stories.article:
The Tamil Nadu government on Tuesday approached the Supreme Court challenging a Madras High Court order related to the Karthigai Deepam dispute, escalating a high-profile legal battle over one of the state's most revered religious festivals. The government's urgent petition signals that the state considers the High Court's direction unworkable or unacceptable, and is seeking the apex court's intervention before the dispute deepens.

The Karthigai Deepam festival, celebrated at the Arunachaleswarar temple in Thiruvannamalai, involves the lighting of a giant flame atop the hill, an act considered sacred by millions of devotees. The controversy involves overlapping claims over faith, property rights, and public order — with the state government and religious stakeholders at odds over who controls key aspects of the ritual and the land associated with it.

The Tamil Nadu government, led by the DMK administration, has consistently argued that the state has a legitimate role in managing large public gatherings and the logistics of major temple festivals. The High Court's order appears to have constrained that authority in ways the state finds untenable, prompting it to seek a higher forum.

The Supreme Court's eventual ruling could have far-reaching implications for how state governments across India exercise oversight over temple administration and religious events, touching on the delicate constitutional balance between the right to religious practice and the state's power to regulate public affairs.
key_facts: Tamil Nadu government challenged a Madras High Court order in the Supreme Court
Row concerns the Karthigai Deepam festival at Arunachaleswarar temple, Thiruvannamalai
Dispute involves faith, property rights, and public order
DMK state government is the petitioner before the Supreme Court

stories.headline: Supreme Court's Footpath Order Comes as India's Pedestrian Deaths Surge
stories.what: The Supreme Court has passed an order affirming the right of pedestrians to walk on footpaths, a ruling that comes against the backdrop of a significant rise in pedestrian deaths on Indian roads. The Indian Express reports that the surge in fatalities — driven by encroachment of footpaths by vehicles, vendors, and illegal construction — has made walking on Indian streets increasingly dangerous. The apex court's intervention highlights the growing urban safety crisis facing ordinary citizens.
stories.lens: For millions of Indians who walk to work every day, the Supreme Court's order directly addresses a life-and-death reality — encroached or non-existent footpaths force pedestrians onto roads where fatal accidents are rising sharply.
stories.article:
The Supreme Court has issued an order asserting the right of citizens to walk on footpaths, a significant judicial intervention that comes as India grapples with a surge in pedestrian deaths on its roads. The ruling underscores the apex court's growing concern over urban infrastructure failures that endanger the lives of the country's most vulnerable road users.

The Indian Express, reporting on the context behind the order, highlights that pedestrian fatalities have been climbing sharply, driven by the systematic encroachment of footpaths by parked vehicles, street vendors, and unauthorised construction. In many Indian cities, footpaths either do not exist or are rendered unusable, pushing walkers onto busy carriageways where they face deadly traffic.

The Supreme Court's order places the onus on civic authorities and state governments to ensure that footpaths are cleared, maintained, and kept accessible. It frames the right to walk safely as a fundamental aspect of the right to life, giving it constitutional heft and opening the door to contempt proceedings against officials who fail to comply.

Urban planning experts and road safety advocates have long flagged pedestrian infrastructure as the most neglected element of Indian cities' transport systems. The court's intervention is expected to put fresh pressure on municipalities across the country to act — though implementation, as with many Supreme Court directives on civic matters, remains the critical test.
key_facts: Supreme Court issued an order affirming citizens' right to walk on footpaths
India has seen a surge in pedestrian deaths on roads
Encroachment by vehicles and vendors renders most footpaths unusable
Ruling frames safe pedestrian access as part of the right to life

stories.headline: Supreme Court Revisits Hadiya Case: 'She Is an Adult, Her Marriage Cannot Be Questioned'
stories.what: The Supreme Court has reiterated that Hadiya, the Kerala woman at the centre of a landmark 2017 conversion and marriage case, is an adult whose marriage cannot be questioned. The case, which involved Hadiya's conversion to Islam and subsequent marriage, had earlier resulted in a significant Supreme Court ruling upholding her autonomy. The court's latest remarks reaffirm the foundational principle that an adult Indian citizen has the absolute right to choose their religion and spouse.
stories.lens: The Hadiya case remains a touchstone for personal liberty in India — the court's reaffirmation that an adult's marriage and faith choices are beyond legal challenge is a powerful reminder of constitutional protections against family or state interference.
stories.article:
The Supreme Court on Tuesday reaffirmed that Hadiya, the Kerala woman whose conversion to Islam and subsequent marriage sparked a nationally watched legal battle, is an adult whose marriage cannot be questioned. The court's remarks revisit one of the most consequential personal liberty cases in recent Indian legal history, restating the principle that an adult citizen's choices of faith and partner are beyond judicial or familial interference.

The Hadiya case first came to national attention when her father approached the Kerala High Court challenging her conversion and marriage to Shafin Jahan. The High Court had annulled the marriage, a decision that drew widespread criticism from civil liberties advocates. The Supreme Court subsequently stepped in, and in its landmark 2017 ruling, upheld Hadiya's autonomy, restored the marriage, and laid down that the state and courts have no business second-guessing an adult's intimate and religious choices.

The court's latest remarks appear to arise from continuing proceedings in the matter, and serve as a firm restatement of its earlier position. By declaring that Hadiya's adult status makes her marriage unchallengeable, the Supreme Court reinforces the constitutional right to personal liberty enshrined in Article 21.

The case has had lasting significance beyond the individuals involved, shaping how Indian courts approach questions of interfaith marriages, religious conversion, and the limits of parental or governmental authority over adult citizens' private lives.
key_facts: Supreme Court said Hadiya is an adult and her marriage cannot be questioned
Hadiya's conversion to Islam and marriage to Shafin Jahan sparked the original case
Kerala High Court had earlier annulled the marriage; Supreme Court restored it in 2017
Case is a landmark on personal liberty, religious freedom, and right to marry

stories.headline: Supreme Court Declines Urgent Hearing on Ration Denial Linked to Special Intensive Revision
stories.what: The Supreme Court has declined to grant an urgent hearing in a petition concerning the denial of ration benefits linked to the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) process. The SIR — a voter roll revision exercise — has been at the centre of controversy, with petitioners alleging that people are being denied rations because their names were removed from electoral rolls during the process. The apex court's refusal to hear the matter urgently means affected individuals may have to wait longer for relief.
stories.lens: For poor families dependent on PDS rations for daily food, any link between electoral roll deletions and ration card eligibility is an immediate threat to food security — making the Supreme Court's decision on urgency directly consequential.
stories.article:
The Supreme Court has declined to grant an urgent hearing in a case alleging that individuals are being denied ration benefits as a result of the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) process — an electoral roll revision exercise that has sparked significant controversy in recent weeks. The court's decision not to fast-track the hearing means petitioners must wait for a regular listing.

The SIR process, which involves intensive verification and revision of voter lists, has been criticised by opposition parties and civil society groups who allege that large numbers of eligible voters — particularly from marginalised communities — have had their names deleted. The petition before the Supreme Court appears to go a step further, claiming that these deletions are now being used as grounds to deny ration entitlements under the public distribution system.

The denial of rations linked to electoral rolls raises serious questions about the intersection of two distinct systems — one governing food security and the other governing democratic participation. Critics argue that conflating the two puts vulnerable populations in double jeopardy: losing both their vote and their access to subsidised food.

By declining urgency, the Supreme Court has signalled that the matter, while significant, does not rise to the level requiring immediate intervention. Petitioners are expected to press for an early date, and the case will be watched closely given the broader political and humanitarian dimensions of the SIR controversy.
key_facts: Supreme Court declined urgent hearing on ration denial linked to SIR
SIR (Special Intensive Revision) is an electoral roll revision exercise
Petitioners allege ration benefits denied to those whose names were removed from voter rolls
Case raises concerns about food security for marginalised communities

stories.headline: Meta halts employee tracking programme for AI training over privacy fears
stories.what: Meta has paused a programme that tracked its workers' activities for the purpose of training artificial intelligence systems, after the initiative triggered significant privacy concerns. The BBC reported that the halt follows internal and external discomfort with using workplace monitoring data to feed AI models. The episode highlights growing tension between the voracious data appetite of large AI developers and the privacy rights of employees — a debate increasingly relevant as companies worldwide push to harvest more data for model training.
stories.lens: As Indian IT companies and AI startups explore similar workplace data practices, Meta's forced pause is a warning that employee privacy concerns can quickly become a hard limit on how companies collect AI training data.
stories.article:
Meta has halted an internal programme that monitored its own employees' work activities to generate data for AI training, the BBC reported, after the initiative drew sharp privacy objections from staff and observers.

The programme reportedly involved tracking worker behaviour — the specifics of what was monitored have not been fully disclosed — with the data intended to improve Meta's AI systems. The company's decision to pause the effort signals that even the world's largest AI developers face real limits when it comes to sourcing training data from their own workforce, a reminder that consent and privacy law apply even inside corporate walls.

The move comes amid a broader industry scramble for high-quality training data. With publicly available internet data increasingly exhausted or litigated over, tech companies have explored synthetic data, licensed content, and internal data sources. Using employee-generated data is a logical extension of that search — but it runs headlong into workplace privacy regulations in multiple jurisdictions, including Europe's GDPR and India's evolving Digital Personal Data Protection framework.

For Indian technology and AI companies watching the global frontier closely, Meta's retreat is instructive. As domestic firms build their own AI training pipelines, the episode underscores that employee data cannot be quietly repurposed without clear consent frameworks — a lesson likely to shape both corporate policy and regulatory guidance in India.
key_facts: Meta paused a programme tracking employees for AI training data
Halt triggered by privacy concerns, per BBC reporting
Highlights tension between AI data needs and employee privacy rights

stories.headline: AI startup sues US government over export curbs blocking access to Anthropic's advanced models
stories.what: An AI startup has filed a lawsuit against the United States government, challenging export restrictions that have cut off access to Anthropic's advanced AI models for customers outside the US. The plaintiff, identified elsewhere as Fable AI — an Anthropic customer — argues that the restrictions unlawfully deprive it of access to tools central to its business. The case is one of the first direct legal challenges to the US government's AI export control regime and could set a precedent for how Washington regulates the global spread of frontier AI technology.
stories.lens: US export controls on advanced AI models directly affect Indian companies and developers who rely on services like Anthropic's Claude — a legal challenge to those curbs could reshape what AI tools are available in India.
stories.article:
A US-based AI startup has sued the American government over export restrictions that blocked its access to Anthropic's advanced AI models, in what appears to be one of the first court challenges to Washington's tightening grip on frontier AI exports.

The plaintiff, identified in reports as Fable AI, an Anthropic customer, argues that the export curbs have effectively destroyed its ability to operate by cutting off access to the advanced models it depends on. The lawsuit targets the legal basis of the restrictions, questioning whether the government has the authority to impose such sweeping controls on AI software access in this manner.

The case lands at a sensitive moment in US technology policy. The Biden and Trump administrations have both moved aggressively to restrict the export of cutting-edge AI capabilities, framing the controls as a national security necessity to prevent adversaries — particularly China — from accessing frontier models. But critics argue the rules are too broad, sweeping up legitimate commercial customers alongside genuine security threats.

For Indian businesses and developers, the outcome carries real stakes. Many startups and enterprises here access Anthropic, OpenAI, and other frontier model providers through US-based APIs. A ruling that narrows export restrictions could ease access; a ruling upholding them could signal further tightening. The case is set to become a landmark test of how AI is governed at the international frontier.
key_facts: AI startup Fable AI sued the US government over Anthropic model export curbs
Lawsuit challenges legality of export restrictions on advanced AI models
Could set precedent for global access to US frontier AI technology

stories.headline: China reclaims world supercomputer crown from US for first time since 2017
stories.what: China has overtaken the United States to claim the top position in global supercomputing rankings for the first time since 2017, according to Business Standard. The development is a significant marker in the intensifying US-China technology rivalry, with supercomputing power directly underpinning advances in artificial intelligence, defence, climate modelling, and scientific research. The shift signals that despite sweeping US chip export controls targeting China, Beijing has managed to develop sufficient homegrown computing muscle to reach the global summit.
stories.lens: Supercomputing power is the backbone of AI development — China retaking the top spot means it now has the raw infrastructure to train the world's most powerful AI models, a shift with direct consequences for India's own AI ambitions and strategic positioning.
stories.article:
China has seized the title of world's leading supercomputing nation from the United States for the first time since 2017, a milestone that underscores Beijing's determination to dominate the hardware layer of the global AI race.

The achievement is particularly striking given the sustained US effort to restrict China's access to advanced semiconductors. Washington has imposed successive rounds of chip export controls specifically designed to limit China's ability to build cutting-edge computing infrastructure. The fact that China has nonetheless reached the top of the supercomputing rankings suggests either that its domestic chip industry has matured faster than US policymakers anticipated, or that it has found ways to work around the restrictions at scale.

Supercomputing supremacy carries direct strategic weight. The fastest machines are used to train the largest and most capable AI models, to simulate nuclear and weapons systems, to crack complex cryptographic problems, and to accelerate pharmaceutical and materials research. A nation that leads in supercomputing has a structural advantage across virtually every technology frontier.

For India, the development is a prompt for reflection. The country has invested in its own high-performance computing infrastructure and has articulated AI as a national strategic priority. But the gap between India's compute capacity and that of the US-China duopoly remains wide. As the AI era makes raw computing power ever more decisive, closing that gap will be one of the central challenges of India's technology policy in the years ahead.
key_facts: China tops global supercomputing rankings for first time since 2017
Surpasses the United States despite US chip export controls
Supercomputing power is critical infrastructure for frontier AI development
stories.image_credit: Argonne National Laboratory's Flickr page / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Anthropic's Mythos AI model found vulnerabilities in classified US government systems
stories.what: Anthropic's Mythos AI model has reportedly uncovered vulnerabilities in classified United States government systems, according to a US official cited by the Associated Press. The revelation marks a significant milestone in the deployment of frontier AI for national security purposes, demonstrating that advanced AI models are now being used in sensitive, high-stakes government environments. The development raises urgent questions about both the capabilities of modern AI and the security implications of deploying such systems on classified infrastructure.
stories.lens: An AI model probing classified government networks for security flaws signals that frontier AI is now a live tool in national security — with direct implications for how India and other governments approach their own AI security frameworks.
stories.article:
Anthropic's Mythos AI model has successfully identified vulnerabilities in classified US government systems, a US official confirmed to the Associated Press — marking one of the most consequential public disclosures of AI being used on sensitive government infrastructure to date.

The finding underscores a dramatic shift in how frontier AI models are being deployed. Rather than serving purely as productivity or research tools, systems like Mythos are now being directed at America's most sensitive networks, tasked with finding the security gaps that human analysts or conventional tools might miss. That an AI identified real vulnerabilities in classified environments suggests the technology has crossed a meaningful threshold in both capability and trust.

The disclosure comes at a turbulent moment for Anthropic. The company is simultaneously at the centre of a separate legal dispute over US export restrictions that have blocked customers from accessing its advanced AI models abroad. Together, the two developments paint a picture of a company whose technology is deeply embedded in US national security considerations — both as a tool the government wants to use and as an asset it wants to control.

For the broader AI industry, the Mythos episode is likely to accelerate debate about governance: who authorises AI to probe classified systems, who is liable if something goes wrong, and whether adversarial nations can reverse-engineer capabilities from such disclosures. India's own nascent AI security programmes will be watching closely.
key_facts: Anthropic's Mythos AI model found vulnerabilities in classified US government systems
Confirmed by a US official cited by the Associated Press
Marks one of the first public disclosures of AI probing classified infrastructure

stories.headline: 'Kicking down the AI ladder': How advanced nations are pulling up AI access behind them
stories.what: The New Indian Express has published an opinion piece arguing that wealthy, technologically advanced nations are deliberately restricting developing countries' access to artificial intelligence tools and infrastructure — effectively 'kicking down the ladder' after climbing it themselves. The commentary draws on the wave of US AI export controls and allied nations' moves to limit AI diffusion as evidence of a new form of technological protectionism. For India, which is seeking to build a domestic AI ecosystem while still depending heavily on foreign models and compute, the argument carries pointed relevance.
stories.lens: If advanced nations lock down AI exports and compute access, India's ambition to become a global AI power could be structurally hobbled — making the geopolitics of AI access one of the most consequential policy questions facing New Delhi.
stories.article:
A sharp opinion piece in The New Indian Express argues that the world's leading AI powers are systematically denying developing nations the tools they need to build competitive artificial intelligence capabilities — repeating a historical pattern in which powerful nations restrict the very technologies that enabled their own rise.

The 'kicking down the ladder' metaphor, borrowed from development economics, captures a dynamic now playing out in AI: the United States has moved aggressively to restrict exports of frontier AI models and advanced semiconductors, while its allies have followed suit with their own controls. The cumulative effect, the commentary argues, is to concentrate cutting-edge AI capability in a small number of wealthy nations and lock everyone else out.

For India, the stakes are immediate. The country's AI ambitions — articulated through programmes like IndiaAI and the push for sovereign compute — rest on the assumption that Indian developers can access frontier models, cloud infrastructure, and chip supply chains. Export controls that tighten around any of those chokepoints could force India into a slower, more expensive path to AI capability, or push it closer to Chinese technology ecosystems as an alternative.

The debate is not merely theoretical. As the Fable AI lawsuit against the US government over Anthropic export restrictions shows, the friction between AI nationalism and global access is already producing legal and commercial conflict. How India navigates that tension — as a consumer of foreign AI, a builder of domestic alternatives, and a voice in global governance forums — will shape its technology trajectory for a generation.
key_facts: Advanced nations accused of restricting AI access to developing countries
US export controls on AI models and chips cited as key mechanism
Directly threatens India's AI development ambitions and sovereign compute goals

stories.headline: FIFA World Cup 2026: latest scores and full results
stories.what: The latest FIFA World Cup 2026 results in full — scorelines, goalscorers and what's coming up next, all in IST.
stories.lens: Every matchday, tracked in your time zone.
stories.article:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 group stage continued with another full matchday. Here are the latest results in full:

England 0-0 Ghana (Group L). Half-time: 0-0. Venue: Boston (Foxborough).

Panama 0-1 Croatia (Group L). Scorers: Ante Budimir 54'. Half-time: 0-0. Venue: Toronto.

France 3-0 Iraq (Group I). Scorers: Kylian Mbappé 14', Kylian Mbappé 54', Ousmane Dembélé 66'. Half-time: 1-0. Venue: Philadelphia.

Norway 3-2 Senegal (Group I). Scorers: Marcus Holmgren Pedersen 43', Erling Haaland 48', Erling Haaland 58', Ismaïla Sarr 53', Ismaïla Sarr 90+3'. Half-time: 1-0. Venue: New York/New Jersey (East Rutherford).

Jordan 1-2 Algeria (Group J). Scorers: Nizar Al-Rashdan 36', Nadhir Benbouali 69', Amine Gouiri 82'. Half-time: 1-0. Venue: San Francisco Bay Area (Santa Clara).

Coming up next (all times IST):

Colombia v DR Congo — 24 Jun, 07:30 AM IST (Group K)

Czech Republic v Mexico — 25 Jun, 06:30 AM IST (Group A)

South Africa v South Korea — 25 Jun, 06:30 AM IST (Group A)

Switzerland v Canada — 25 Jun, 12:30 AM IST (Group B)

Full group standings, scorers and the complete fixture list are on our FIFA World Cup 2026 page, updated through the day.
key_facts: England 0-0 Ghana
Panama 0-1 Croatia
France 3-0 Iraq
Norway 3-2 Senegal

stories.headline: Kapil Dev likens young Vaibhav Suryavanshi to a 16-year-old Sachin Tendulkar — with a caveat
stories.what: Cricket legend Kapil Dev has drawn a remarkable comparison between teenage batting prodigy Vaibhav Suryavanshi and a young Sachin Tendulkar, saying he is reminiscent of a 16-year-old Tendulkar. However, Kapil Dev stopped short of an unconditional endorsement, adding a qualifying note about whether Suryavanshi can sustain and build on his early promise. The comparison is one of the most high-profile endorsements yet of the young batsman's extraordinary talent.
stories.lens: When Kapil Dev — who played alongside Tendulkar at the start of Sachin's career — invokes that comparison, it sets the scale of expectation on young Suryavanshi and signals just how seriously the cricketing establishment is watching him.
stories.article:
Kapil Dev, India's 1983 World Cup-winning captain, has offered a striking assessment of teenage batting sensation Vaibhav Suryavanshi, likening him to a 16-year-old Sachin Tendulkar — arguably the most flattering comparison any young Indian cricketer could receive.

The former all-rounder, who famously shared the Indian dressing room during the early years of Tendulkar's legendary career, said the similarities are visible in the way Suryavanshi approaches his batting at such a young age. The raw talent, the instinct, and the fearlessness appear to have drawn Kapil Dev's eye in a way that few young players do.

However, Kapil Dev attached an important qualifier to his praise, stopping short of a blanket guarantee and posing the central question of whether Suryavanshi can back up his early brilliance over the long haul. It is a caveat that reflects the hard-won wisdom of a man who has seen many talents burst onto the scene only to fade — and a small number go on to truly fulfil their promise.

Suryavanshi has been turning heads in domestic and age-group cricket, and Kapil Dev's comments are likely to intensify scrutiny on the youngster. For Indian cricket fans, the comparison to a young Tendulkar is both thrilling and a reminder of the enormous expectations that now rest on his shoulders.
key_facts: Kapil Dev compared Suryavanshi to a 16-year-old Sachin Tendulkar
Kapil raised doubts about whether Suryavanshi can sustain his form
Suryavanshi is a teenage batting prodigy generating widespread buzz

stories.headline: Croatia beat Panama 1-0 to advance, eliminate Central Americans from FIFA World Cup
stories.what: Croatia secured a crucial 1-0 victory over Panama at the FIFA World Cup, with Andrej Budimir scoring the decisive winner to keep Croatia's tournament hopes alive. The result eliminates Panama from the competition. The match also marked a landmark occasion for veteran midfielder Luka Modrić. Croatia's clinical win underlines their tournament resilience despite a difficult group stage.
stories.lens: For Indian football fans following the World Cup, Croatia's Modrić-era farewell run remains one of the tournament's most compelling storylines — and this result keeps it alive.
stories.article:
Croatia eliminated Panama from the FIFA World Cup with a hard-fought 1-0 victory, Andrej Budimir's strike proving to be the difference between the two sides. The win was vital for the Croatian side, who needed the points to stay in contention in a competitive group, while Panama's tournament campaign came to an end with the defeat.

Budimir's goal was the solitary moment of quality that separated the teams in what was a tense contest. Croatia, perennial over-achievers on the world stage, once again demonstrated the tournament experience that has defined their runs in recent editions of the World Cup.

The match also carried additional emotional weight as it was described as a landmark occasion for Luka Modrić, the talismanic Croatian captain whose World Cup appearances are increasingly precious. Modrić's presence continues to elevate Croatia above their weight class on the international stage.

Panama, competing with characteristic effort, could not find the equaliser that would have extended their campaign. Their exit marks the end of a spirited but ultimately unsuccessful World Cup run for the Central American side. Croatia now look ahead to their next fixture with renewed momentum.
key_facts: Croatia beat Panama 1-0
Andrej Budimir scored the winning goal
Panama eliminated from the FIFA World Cup
Match was a landmark occasion for Luka Modrić

stories.headline: Ellyse Perry's all-round show hammers Pakistan, sets up Australia vs India Women's clash
stories.what: Australia's Ellyse Perry produced an all-round performance to help her side demolish Pakistan, setting up a high-stakes match against India in the women's competition. Perry's contribution with both bat and ball underlined why she remains one of the most complete cricketers in the women's game. The win puts Australia in prime position ahead of what promises to be a marquee contest against India.
stories.lens: An Australia vs India women's clash is always one of the biggest fixtures in women's cricket, and Perry firing into form ahead of it makes the contest even more compelling for Indian fans.
stories.article:
Ellyse Perry underscored her status as one of women's cricket's finest all-rounders with a dominant display against Pakistan, helping Australia to a comprehensive victory that sets up a highly anticipated meeting with India. Perry's contributions across both disciplines of the game were central to Australia's commanding win.

The result eliminates Pakistan as a threat and means Australia arrive at the India fixture brimming with confidence. For India, the knowledge that Perry is in fine form will be a concern — she has been a consistent match-winner against them over the years and represents arguably the most dangerous individual player they will face.

Australia have long been the benchmark in women's cricket and their clinical performance against Pakistan reinforced that status. The manner of the win, with Perry leading the way, suggests the defending champions are peaking at the right moment of the tournament.

The forthcoming India-Australia tie is shaping up to be one of the tournament's standout fixtures. India will need their own all-round performances and sharp plans for Perry if they are to prevent Australia from continuing their march through the competition.
key_facts: Ellyse Perry starred with an all-round performance against Pakistan
Australia beat Pakistan to set up a match against India
Perry's form a concern for India ahead of the fixture

stories.headline: England held to goalless draw by Ghana, raising World Cup qualification concerns
stories.what: England were held to a goalless draw by Ghana in a FIFA World Cup fixture, a result that has exposed worrying signs about the Three Lions' form and their ability to progress in the tournament. It was England's 13th goalless draw in World Cup history, a statistic that underlines a recurring pattern of frustration for one of football's storied nations. The draw has intensified scrutiny on England's attacking output and tactical approach.
stories.lens: For Indian fans following the World Cup, England's inability to break down Ghana is a reminder of the tournament's unpredictability — and a sign that no team, however pedigreed, is guaranteed to cruise through.
stories.article:
England were frustrated by Ghana in a goalless draw at the FIFA World Cup, a result that has reignited concerns about the side's ability to find goals and build momentum in the competition. The 0-0 scoreline was England's 13th World Cup goalless draw, a recurring feature of their tournament history that supporters and pundits find hard to ignore.

England came into the match as heavy favourites against the Ghanaian side, but found themselves unable to convert possession and territorial advantage into goals. Ghana defended resolutely and denied England the breakthrough that would have settled nerves and put the Three Lions in control of their group destiny.

The result has exposed what ESPN India described as England's "World Cup run worries" — a familiar anxiety about a team blessed with individual talent but prone to collective underperformance on the biggest stage. The draw means England must sharpen their attacking intent quickly if they are to advance deep into the tournament.

For Ghana, the point represents a creditable result against one of Europe's most fancied sides and keeps their own campaign alive. England's coaching staff now face difficult questions about selection and tactics as the stakes in the World Cup rise with every passing game.
key_facts: England drew 0-0 with Ghana at the FIFA World Cup
England's 13th World Cup goalless draw
Result raises concerns about England's scoring form

stories.headline: Sanjay Manjrekar: Selection must prioritise Indian cricket over individual stars like Kohli and Rohit
stories.what: Former India cricketer and commentator Sanjay Manjrekar has weighed into the debate around senior players' futures, asserting that selectors must always prioritise what is best for Indian cricket rather than what is best for any individual player, including Virat Kohli and Rohit Sharma. His comments reflect a growing chorus from the cricket establishment urging a more objective, merit-based selection approach. The remarks come amid intense public discussion about how long India's senior stars should continue in international cricket.
stories.lens: With the 2027 ODI World Cup selection debate heating up, Manjrekar's call for cricket-first selection over loyalty to star names directly shapes the conversation about who India will field at the next global tournament.
stories.article:
Sanjay Manjrekar has added a prominent voice to the debate over India's selection future, stating plainly that decisions must always be made in the best interests of Indian cricket — and not in favour of any individual, however legendary, including Virat Kohli and Rohit Sharma.

Manjrekar's remarks, published in The New Indian Express, reflect a sentiment that has been gaining ground among former cricketers and analysts as the careers of India's two most high-profile senior batsmen enter their twilight phase. The argument is a simple but politically sensitive one: at some point, the team's needs must come before the reputations of its biggest stars.
